source.title: Rivaroxaban in Peripheral Artery Disease after Revascularization
source.organization: The New England Journal of Medicine

extraction.summary: This randomized controlled trial investigated rivaroxaban 2.5 mg twice daily plus aspirin versus placebo plus aspirin in patients with peripheral artery disease after lower-extremity revascularization. The combination of rivaroxaban plus aspirin significantly reduced the composite outcome of acute limb ischemia, major amputation, myocardial infarction, ischemic stroke, or cardiovascular death by 15% (HR 0.85, 95% CI 0.76-0.96, P=0.009).

population.risk_criteria: Lower-extremity peripheral artery disease with successful revascularization within previous 10 days
population.disease_definition: Documented lower-extremity peripheral artery disease with symptoms, anatomical evidence, and hemodynamic evidence; majority had history of claudication (96%) with median ankle-brachial index 0.56
population.exclusions: Clinically unstable condition, heightened bleeding risk, taking prohibited concomitant medications including long-term clopidogrel
population.severity_staging: 23% had critical limb ischemia at index revascularization; 65% underwent endovascular procedures, 35% surgical

key_thresholds.context: Rivaroxaban vs placebo groups, Kaplan-Meier estimates
Rivaroxaban vs placebo, HR 1.43 (95% CI 0.97-2.10), P=0.07
Rivaroxaban vs placebo, HR 1.42 (95% CI 1.10-1.84), P=0.007

recommendations.recommendation: Rivaroxaban 2.5 mg twice daily plus aspirin reduces major adverse limb and cardiovascular events in peripheral artery disease patients after revascularization
recommendations.notes: 15% relative risk reduction with acceptable bleeding risk profile

recommendations.recommendation: Monitor for increased ISTH major bleeding risk with combination therapy
recommendations.notes: Significant increase in ISTH major bleeding but no excess in intracranial or fatal bleeding

recommendations.recommendation: Consider adding rivaroxaban 2.5mg twice daily to aspirin in patients with symptomatic peripheral artery disease after lower-extremity revascularization
recommendations.notes: Reduces adverse limb and cardiovascular events with acceptable bleeding risk

recommendations.recommendation: Monitor for bleeding events, particularly ISTH major bleeding which was increased
recommendations.notes: No significant increase in fatal bleeding or intracranial hemorrhage

recommendations.recommendation: Benefits extend current guideline recommendations for aspirin or clopidogrel monotherapy
recommendations.notes: Complements findings from COMPASS trial in broader peripheral artery disease population

medications.indication: Prevention of adverse limb and cardiovascular events in PAD post-revascularization
Prevention of ischemic events, stable coronary disease, acute coronary syndromes

red_flags: ISTH major bleeding significantly increased (HR 1.42, P=0.007)
Intracranial hemorrhage occurred in 13 rivaroxaban vs 17 placebo patients
Fatal bleeding occurred in 6 patients in each group
Adverse events were leading cause of early discontinuation
ISTH major bleeding increased significantly (HR 1.42, p=0.007)
High treatment discontinuation rates (~14% per year)
BARC major bleeding grade 3b or greater (HR 1.29, p=0.10)
Similar rates of serious adverse events leading to discontinuation (20 vs 18 patients)
Major bleeding mentioned as clinical investigation endpoint
extraction.follow_up: Median 28 months follow-up (IQR 22-34 months); event-driven trial with 98.8% complete ascertainment of primary outcome; vital status available for 99.6% of patients
extraction.escalation_path: Results support use of rivaroxaban plus aspirin in peripheral artery disease patients post-revascularization given significant reduction in major adverse events with acceptable bleeding risk. Findings are generalizable across subgroups and consistent with COMPASS trial results. Clinical implementation should consider individual bleeding risk assessment.
